stem: What is the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \) is divided by \( [[m]] \)?
solution: The powers of \( [[a]] \) modulo \( [[m]] \) are periodic; find the cycle length, reduce the exponent \( [[b]] \) modulo that length, and read off the residue. The remainder is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses exponent \( [[b]]+1 \), one step too far around the cycle.
This uses exponent \( [[b]]-1 \), one step short in the cycle.
This is \( [[a]] \bmod [[m]] \), the residue of the base alone.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.
This is three more than the correct remainder.
This is \( [[a]]^{2} \bmod [[m]] \), a mid-cycle value rather than the value at exponent \( [[b]] \).

stem: The powers of \( [[a]] \) modulo 29 repeat in a cycle: \( [[a]]^{1}, [[a]]^{2}, [[a]]^{3}, \dots \pmod{29} \). What is the length of this cycle, i.e. the smallest positive integer \( t \) such that \( [[a]]^{t} \equiv 1 \pmod{29} \)?
solution: Since 2 is a primitive root mod 29, it has order 28, and \( [[a]] = 2^{[[k]]} \) has order \( 28/\gcd([[k]],28) = [[answer]] \) modulo 29.
distractor_rationales: This is the order of 2 itself (a primitive root), not of \( [[a]]=2^{[[k]]} \).
This is \( \gcd([[k]],28) \), the reciprocal factor, not the order itself.
This is the exponent \( k \) used to build \( [[a]] \), not its order.
This is one more than the correct cycle length.
This is one less than the correct cycle length.
This is two more than the correct cycle length.
This doubles the correct cycle length.

stem: The powers of \( [[a]] \) modulo 29 repeat with cycle length \( [[cyc]] \). Among \( [[a]]^{1}, [[a]]^{2}, \dots, [[a]]^{[[N]]} \), how many are congruent to \( 1 \pmod{29} \)?
solution: \( [[a]]^{t}\equiv 1\pmod{29} \) exactly when \( [[cyc]] \) divides \( t \); the number of multiples of \( [[cyc]] \) from 1 to \( [[N]] \) is \( \lfloor [[N]]/[[cyc]] \rfloor = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the cycle length itself, not the count of hits within \( [[N]] \) terms.
This divides by 28 (the order of the generator 2) instead of the actual cycle length of \( [[a]] \).
This rounds up instead of down, potentially overcounting when \( [[cyc]] \) does not divide \( [[N]] \) evenly.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.
This is two more than the correct count.
This is the complementary count of terms NOT congruent to 1.

stem: Let \( S = 1 + [[a]] + [[a]]^{2} + \cdots + [[a]]^{[[N]]-1} \), a sum of \( [[N]] \) terms. What is the remainder when \( S \) is divided by \( [[m]] \)?
solution: By the geometric series formula, \( S = \dfrac{[[a]]^{[[N]]} - 1}{[[a]] - 1} \), an exact integer. Reducing this modulo \( [[m]] \) gives \( S \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[m]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only the last term of the sum, ignoring all the earlier terms.
This multiplies the term count by the base instead of summing a geometric series.
This reduces the term count itself modulo \( [[m]] \), an unrelated quantity.
This uses one extra term in the geometric series sum.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is one less than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.

stem: The modulus \( [[m]] = [[q]]\cdot[[r]] \) shares the factor \( [[q]] \) with the base \( [[a]] = [[q]]\cdot[[t]] \), so the powers of \( [[a]] \) are NOT coprime to \( [[m]] \) and Fermat/Euler-style cycle arguments cannot be applied directly to the whole modulus. What is the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \) is divided by \( [[m]] \)?
solution: Since \( \gcd([[a]],[[m]]) = [[q]] \ne 1 \), reduce mod \( [[q]] \) (where \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \equiv 0 \), as \( [[q]] \) already divides \( [[a]] \)) and mod \( [[r]] \) (where \( [[a]] \) IS coprime to \( [[r]] \), so a genuine cycle applies) separately, then recombine via the Chinese Remainder Theorem: \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[m]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This uses exponent \( [[b]]+1 \), one power too many.
This uses exponent \( [[b]]-1 \), one power too few.
This is \( [[a]] \bmod [[m]] \), the residue of the base alone.
This is \( [[a]]^{2} \bmod [[m]] \), a low power rather than the value at exponent \( [[b]] \).
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is one less than the correct remainder.
This is off from the correct remainder by the shared factor \( [[q]] \).
